item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Unfortunately, both NC and ND are not compliant with CC BY-SA 3.0. As such, the image will be removed.

item.title: You learn something new every day.
item.content:encoded:
I noticed the city named here was also mentioned in another article, and looked it up. Now I know about an economic center in Russia.

But yeah, +1 for influenza spreading kittens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When back here after re-watching an episode on Seven Universe where Steven try to shapeship into something ended up something similar to SCP-1797 except the detached part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great article. Concise and unique. +1.

Also liked the backstory a few posts up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Vsevolod is weak and ill and undernourished, but not enough to kill him. But the Foundation took away his pets, took away his knives and forks and his favorite chair. He created them, he invested himself in them, in spirit and power more than in body. They are a part of him. With so much of him destroyed or taken away, there isn't enough left to will him to live. And so he dies.

…

So he was like a Sauron who, instead of pouring his essence into the One Ring, decided to put into…kittens and things made from kittens. I like this even more now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whenever I see the picture for this one, I think the cat's mouth is moving and I freak out for a second.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am so glad I asked!
That is some detailed and intriguing back-story you've got going on, and I never would have expected it from the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, it conserves mass. If the host doesn't compensate for the process by consuming more nutrients, he'll lose on average 2.5 kg per cycle. Since an average person will only go through 2 or 3 cycles before their immune system overcomes the virus, the loss of mass is usually not fatal or health-threating. Most hosts will naturally feel hungrier as new kittens grow, eating more and reducing the mass loss to a minimum.

Now, if you were already underweight when you got the virus, don't have an easily accessible source of food, or your immune system isn't working properly and you remain infected for several weeks, then you may have a problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tumors will continue to grow for several days, reaching 12-20 cm in size and gradually taking the shape of an instance of SCP-1797-A. Once fully grown, SCP-1797-A will animate and detach from the host's body. The process will repeat for as long as the host is infected with SCP-1797.

So does it create matter from nothing, rather than cannibalizing matter from the host? If so, the article should note that explicitly, and also be labeled with "ectoentropic".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I now want to upvote a second time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to use this question as an excuse to wall-of-text most of my head-canon about Chernodubov. Anyone reading this is free to apply Death of the Author as they see fit; I adore that concept.

The article states that Chernodubov has been breeding cats for 7 years. Being continuously sick with flu for so long, as well as 1797 going around without anyone noticing, may seem like a plot hole; it isn't. The virus is a recent thing, and Chernodubov was never sick. Growing organisms from himself is something that he can do intrinsically. He used to be able to do more, but he is old and weak and tired now. He could grow rabbits or something else instead, even weak as he is, but he chose cats because he likes cats. He could not grow anything, but he never lead what could be called a normal life, always relied on his miracles, and doesn't know how to survive without them.

For the past 7 years, maybe longer, he's been living in this apartment that isn't even his, but the owner is gone and no one else cares enough about that hole to come and evict him. There is no water or electricity. Money is scarce, gained by selling scavenged scrap-metal and kittens. He doesn't get any pension, because while he was born in Nizhnevartovsk, he spent most of his life far away, and there is no documents anywhere in the city to even prove his existence. When someone doesn't want to buy a kitten, Vsevolod may give it away for free; he thinks that it is important to have a pet, and enjoys seeing other people leave with his spawn. The scrap is hard to gather and doesn't sell that well, so for the past few years Vsevolod has being staying in the apartment, going out only infrequently, once in two or three or four months perhaps. As time goes on, he stays in his little corner of the world for longer and longer, going out less and less.

To survive, Vsevolod grows cats. It costs him strength and flesh, but once they are complete, they can leave the apartment, find some food, rats maybe or some passer-by will throw them something; and then they return to him. His diet is monotonous and unhealthy, but he survives. The cycle continues for years: cats are born, they spread and scavenge for food, and then they return to provide him with nourishment and matter to create more of them. Even so, the cats mostly catch little, and something always left in bones and hides. Vsevolod is always hungry, and always weak.

One day, one of the cats gets sick. Cats are not supposed to catch flu, but these are closer to a human than most. From the first kitten, the rest are soon infected. In cats-that-aren't-cats, the virus changes and mutates, and eventually learns to do what Vsevolod does. Soon there are kittens growing from everyone and people running in circles in panic. The Foundation swoops in, exterminates stray cats, administers amnestics, and everything is orderly once again.

Vsevolod is weak and ill and undernourished, but not enough to kill him. But the Foundation took away his pets, took away his knives and forks and his favorite chair. He created them, he invested himself in them, in spirit and power more than in body. They are a part of him. With so much of him destroyed or taken away, there isn't enough left to will him to live. And so he dies. The Foundation is left thinking that he was nothing more than a crazy old man who caught the virus from no one knows where.

Oh hey, this rant is now longer than the article. Anyway, I had an absurd amount of fun creating Chernodubov, maybe as much as from writing the rest of this skip. His name literally means "Lord of everything, son of Nikolai, of black oak". I think I'll write some tales about him some time, about when he was younger and traveled places and met interesting people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm unclear on just one point; was Chernodubov undernourished because he's been over-"breeding"? Or was it because kittens, while tasty, aren't a good source for 100% of your daily allowance of vitamins and minerals?

Because the second seems the more horrendous, but it might be a whole-cloth product of my own screwy head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait, you combined a virus, odd growths on the body, and a fuzzy adorable critter in an SCP and then made it so that it didn't kill the host in a painful and horrific manner?
I'm willing to upvote on merit of that alone…

item.title: Licenses and attributions and stuff
item.content:encoded:
The picture currently used as the illustration for this article was taken by Paolo Margari and is used here under the Creative Commons BY-NC-ND 2.0 License.

All hail Creative Commons!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
so we have tumor-squids, wart-flies, cancer-kitten…
whats next? aids-lizards?

meh. well written but seems like a long row of sets. like in a card game.
itching-frog beats sneezing-worms in strength

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Puts a swing on the crazy cat lady. She's gone viral! I like this. It makes my skin crawl. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, they can make Hello Kitty furniture!

Actually there's a Far side cartoon like that, but with cows. (But I like kitties better.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, when you have a flu that makes you grow hammers…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kitten flu! Fiendish. Also why the man making kitten furniture that is kind of awful. D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I prefer the squid tumors… maybe it's nostalgia, but I feel like they did it better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is insidious and vile. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeelp I'm confused. The part of me that says "OMFG KYOOT AN GOOSHY KITTIES ALL MY PLUS!" is arguing with the part that says "RAWR SCP NOT CUTE AND FUZZY DOWNVOTE!"
In turn, the argument spreads to "You can't downvote a kitty YOU HEARTLESS BASTARD CAKE!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the hell did I just read.
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, alright.
Didn't notice that before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That the virus is transmited through contact with -As is already stated in the very first sentence of the description.

The justifications for why the containment procedures are the way they are have in-universe no place in the brief summary that is the SCP Report.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well then put it in there, would you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like kittens, plain and simple. On the other side, The hook took me from my neck with that guy selling and using kittens for furniture. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, and I'm glad Drewbear pointed out the tableware/furniture point. I feel dense having missed it.

However, I still feel like this one doesn't quite live up to its own potential as an idea. It's short, and it's at least partially effective in its brevity (I'm not left with any loose ends) but my curiosity is piqued and I want to know more about it.

So close to an upvote, but I'm going to hold back for now…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The core concept ("kitten flu") is surreal in that "uh, what?" way that confuses me rather than impresses me, but the fact that this guy was making furniture out of kittens that grew from his own body is pretty damn horrific.

I mean, think about how small a kitten is. Now think about how many kitten spines it would take to make a single table leg. Now think about how many times he had to "birth" new product. Undernourishment, indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the pragmatical approach patient zero took. Kittens popping from my goddamn body? No problem, I'll sell them for spare cash and make furniture out of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kitten tumors? Sure, why not. Plus one.

I'd cut the image, though. It doesn't add anything to the article and I hate it when a picture spoils a good twist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They do transmit the disease, in the same way other animals transmit normal flu strains. The kittens are sedated so that they wouldn't run around and scratch things, potentially compromising the biosafety procedures and infecting researchers with the virus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aww, it's influenza that makes kitties!

But serious time now: it's just a disease that creates small (somewhat adorable) creatures. Is it really all that dangerous? Is it really necessary to sedate the kittens? They don't seem dangerous. Might make more sense if they transmitted the disease themselves somehow.
